title: Napoleon crowned Emperor of the French
summary: On 2 December 1804, in a snow-cold Notre-Dame de Paris hung with imperial eagles, the thirty-five-year-old Corsican general Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor of the French. Pope Pius VII had travelled from Rome to bless the ceremony but Napoleon lifted the diadem from the altar and set it on his own head, then crowned his wife Josephine. The gesture, at once revolutionary and monarchical, closed the fifteen-year cycle opened by the fall of the Bastille and inaugurated an empire that would reshape the map of Europe for a decade.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The French Republic proclaimed in September 1792 had spent a decade lurching from Terror to Directory to Consulate, and by 1804 its most successful general had become its master in all but name. Napoleon Bonaparte, born in Ajaccio in 1769 to minor Corsican nobility, had won a brigadiership at twenty-four by driving the British from Toulon, had subdued northern Italy in 1796 and 1797, had sailed to Egypt in 1798, and had returned in October 1799 to overthrow the Directory in the coup of 18 Brumaire. The Constitution of the Year VIII made him First Consul; a plebiscite in 1802 made him Consul for life; a further constitutional revision in the spring of 1804 turned the Republic into a hereditary empire.

The pretext was security. In February 1804 a royalist conspiracy against Napoleon led by Georges Cadoudal was uncovered in Paris. A month later French dragoons crossed into neutral Baden and seized the Duc d'Enghien, a Bourbon prince distantly implicated in nothing, who was tried by a hasty military commission at Vincennes and shot in the chateau moat on the night of 20 to 21 March. The murder shocked the courts of Europe but consolidated Bonapartist opinion at home. A hereditary imperial dignity, its supporters argued, would put an end to conspiracies against a single mortal life and secure the gains of the Revolution against a Bourbon restoration.

On 18 May 1804 the Senate promulgated the sénatus-consulte that vested the government of the Republic in an emperor of the French, Napoleon Bonaparte. A plebiscite the following autumn returned an implausible three and a half million votes in favour and only some 2,500 against. To root the new order in the deepest reservoir of European legitimacy, Napoleon opened negotiations with Pope Pius VII, who agreed, after weeks of hesitation and quiet bargaining, to travel to Paris in person to consecrate the new emperor. It was the first papal coronation of a Western sovereign since Charlemagne had knelt before Leo III in Saint Peter's on Christmas Day of 800, and Napoleon was determined to be seen as the heir of both traditions.

image.caption: Napoleon Bonaparte as King of Italy, oil on canvas by Andrea Appiani, 1805, Kunsthistorisches Museum Vienna.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Pope reached Fontainebleau on 25 November after a journey of a month from Rome, met the emperor in the forest during a staged hunting party, and was installed at the Pavillon de Flore. Notre-Dame, half-derelict from revolutionary neglect, had been draped in cloth of gold and tricolour bunting under the direction of the architects Percier and Fontaine, its Gothic bays hidden behind a neoclassical proscenium of eagles, garlands and imperial N monograms. A wooden gallery raised the two thrones above the crowd of dignitaries; a second gallery above the western portal held choirs and orchestras.

Sunday 2 December broke bitterly cold and dark. Cannon at the Tuileries and the Invalides woke the city at dawn. The papal procession set out from the palace at nine, followed at eleven by the imperial cortege of some twenty-five carriages escorted by mameluke guards. Napoleon, wearing a long white satin tunic embroidered in gold and a crimson velvet mantle lined with ermine and strewn with golden bees, entered the cathedral shortly before noon to the sound of the Marche du sacre composed by Jean-François Le Sueur. The scepter attributed to Charles V and the Hand of Justice were carried before him. Josephine, in a matching white satin gown, walked behind with the emperor's three sisters, who had been persuaded only at the last moment to carry her train.

The liturgy blended Roman ritual with elements borrowed from the coronation ordo of the medieval French kings. Pius VII anointed Napoleon and Josephine with holy chrism on the forehead and hands. Then, at the moment of the crowning, the emperor removed the laurel wreath he had worn into the cathedral, lifted a new gold circlet made in the shape of the lost Crown of Charlemagne from the altar, and placed it on his own head, then set a small pearl and diamond diadem on the head of his kneeling wife. He had warned the Pope in advance that he would do so; the gesture was as much administrative as insolent, a public assertion that his authority derived from the French people and his own arms rather than from any ecclesiastical grant. After the mass Napoleon swore a civil oath on the Gospels to maintain the territorial integrity of the Republic, the Concordat of 1801, religious freedom, and the Legion of Honour. The whole ceremony had cost more than eight and a half million francs. Fifteen days later at the Champ de Mars he distributed new eagles to his regiments.

image.caption: Jacques-Louis David, The Coronation of Napoleon (1805-1807), Musee du Louvre, Paris.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Within eighteen months the new Empire had transformed the political geography of Europe. In May 1805 Napoleon rode to Milan and, in the Duomo, crowned himself King of Italy with the ancient iron crown of the Lombards. In August the Third Coalition of Britain, Austria and Russia declared war. On 2 December 1805, the first anniversary of the coronation, he destroyed a combined Austro-Russian army at Austerlitz in Moravia. The Peace of Pressburg dissolved the Holy Roman Empire in fact if not in law; on 6 August 1806 the last Habsburg emperor, Francis II, abdicated the thousand-year-old title. A ring of client kingdoms in Naples, Holland, Westphalia and, from 1807, the Duchy of Warsaw carried the Code Napoléon, meritocratic administration, the sale of Church lands and Jewish emancipation into the German and Italian lands.

The imperial nobility created by decree in 1808 grafted a new hierarchy of princes, dukes, counts and barons onto the meritocratic army and administration; Josephine, unable to give the emperor an heir, was set aside in 1809 and replaced in 1810 by the eighteen-year-old Habsburg archduchess Marie-Louise, whose son, the King of Rome, was born on 20 March 1811 in an atmosphere of dynastic triumph.

Yet the imperial construction rested on continuous war. The Continental System of 1806 tried to strangle British trade and drove Napoleon into the disastrous invasion of Spain in 1808 and of Russia in 1812. The retreat from Moscow, the German 'War of Liberation' in 1813, and the invasion of France in 1814 stripped the emperor of allies, marshals and armies. He abdicated at Fontainebleau on 6 April 1814 and was exiled to Elba. The Hundred Days of 1815 ended at Waterloo. The gilded pageant of December 1804, however, had lasting effects: legal codes, administrative departments, secular civil registration, and a memory of national grandeur that would haunt French politics for the rest of the nineteenth century.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The coronation of 2 December 1804 was one of the most consciously choreographed political events of the modern era. Jacques-Louis David, commissioned in 1804 and working from sketches taken inside Notre-Dame, produced between 1805 and 1807 a canvas nearly ten metres long that placed the empress at the compositional centre and depicted the crowning of Josephine rather than Napoleon, a diplomatic edit that spared the Pope's dignity while enshrining the dynastic moment. Exhibited at the Salon of 1808, it now dominates a wall of the Louvre and has become, alongside Ingres's imperial portraits and the arch of the Carrousel, the visual shorthand for the whole Napoleonic idea.

The deeper legacy is institutional. The Empire codified the laws that governed France under every subsequent regime; its prefects still run the departments; its lycées still educate the elite; its Bank of France still issues currency; its Legion of Honour is still pinned to the lapels of soldiers, scientists and writers. Across Europe the Napoleonic administrations left behind cadastral surveys, civil codes and the memory of an efficient state, all of which shaped the reforms of the Restoration and the liberal movements of the nineteenth century.

Politically, the coronation set a precedent that Louis-Napoleon would consciously imitate when he transformed the Second Republic into the Second Empire in 1852, choosing 2 December as the date of both his 1851 coup and his 1852 accession. In popular memory the ceremony fused with the legend of Austerlitz, the return from Elba and the death at Saint Helena in 1821 into the Napoleonic myth that Victor Hugo, Stendhal and Balzac would carry into the literature of the century. Whether the emperor is remembered as the enlightened moderniser of his admirers or the militaristic tyrant of his opponents, the moment when a Corsican general placed a crown on his own head in a Paris cathedral remains one of the boldest symbolic acts in European political history.

image.caption: Empress Josephine in her coronation robes, by Francois Gerard, c. 1807-1808.
image.credit: Francois Gerard, via Google Art Project / Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The United States under President Thomas Jefferson had just concluded the Louisiana Purchase from Napoleon's own government the previous year, doubling its territory for fifteen million dollars (worth roughly 340 million today) and opening the Mississippi valley and the vast unmapped west to eventual settlement; the Third Consul had sold Louisiana after his Saint-Domingue army was destroyed by yellow fever and Haitian resistance. In November 1804 the Corps of Discovery under Meriwether Lewis and William Clark, wintering with the Mandan-Hidatsa at Fort Mandan on the upper Missouri, was preparing (with the interpreter Sacagawea joining that winter) to push west toward the Pacific, which they would reach in November 1805. Further south, the Black republic of Haiti had proclaimed independence from France on 1 January 1804 after Jean-Jacques Dessalines's army defeated the French expeditionary force sent by Napoleon under his brother-in-law Charles Leclerc to reimpose slavery. On 22 September 1804 Dessalines took the title of Emperor Jacques I, an ironic mirror of the ceremony in Paris that would follow ten weeks later. In Spanish America, Simon Bolivar was in Paris that autumn as a young man of twenty-one, witness to Napoleon's coronation, and reportedly disgusted by it.

image.caption: Jean-Jacques Dessalines, revolutionary general who declared Haitian independence on 1 January 1804 and was proclaimed Emperor Jacques I in October 1804. Portrait, 1878.

worldContext.text: The Qing empire under the Jiaqing Emperor (Yongyan), who had ascended in 1796 after his father the Qianlong Emperor's formal abdication but effectively taken power only on the latter's death in 1799, was struggling to suppress the White Lotus Rebellion, a millenarian sectarian uprising in the mountainous borderlands of Sichuan, Hubei, and Shaanxi that had begun in 1794 and would drag on until 1805, exposing serious weaknesses in the Manchu Eight Banner armies and consuming an estimated 200 million taels of silver, roughly five years of imperial revenue. The corrupt grand councillor Heshen, favourite of the old emperor, had been forced to suicide in 1799. Along the south China coast the pirate confederation eventually led by Zheng Yi's widow Ching Shih (Zheng Yi Sao), commanding hundreds of junks and tens of thousands of men, was reaching the height of its power. In Tokugawa Japan the shogun Tokugawa Ienari (r. 1787 to 1837) continued the policy of sakoku, admitting only limited Dutch and Chinese trade through the artificial island of Dejima in Nagasaki harbour; the merchant scholar Ino Tadataka was in the middle of the great cartographic survey of Japan he had begun in 1800.

image.caption: The Jiaqing Emperor (Yongyan, r. 1796 to 1820), whose reign was consumed by the White Lotus Rebellion and by the corruption exposed after Qianlong's death. Palace Museum, Beijing.
image.credit: Unknown Qing court artist, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In India the British East India Company was completing its conquest of the Maratha Confederacy. Under Governor-General Richard Wellesley, elder brother of the future Duke of Wellington, Company armies commanded by Arthur Wellesley and Gerard Lake had crushed the Maratha field forces at the Battles of Assaye (23 September 1803) and Argaon (29 November 1803), and had imposed the Treaty of Surji-Anjangaon on Daulat Rao Scindia in December; the Second Anglo-Maratha War would formally conclude with the humbling of the Holkars in December 1805. Delhi had been taken from the Marathas and its blind Mughal emperor Shah Alam II placed under British protection in September 1803. In Punjab, Ranjit Singh, of the Sukerchakia misl, had unified the twelve Sikh confederacies and taken Lahore in July 1799; he was proclaimed Maharaja of the Punjab on the Baisakhi festival of April 1801, beginning the last independent Indian empire, which would hold the Khyber frontier until his death in 1839. The princely states of Hyderabad under the Nizam and Mysore (defeated with the death of Tipu Sultan in 1799) were now Company allies.

image.caption: The golden throne of Maharaja Ranjit Singh, made by Hafiz Muhammad Multani c. 1820-1830, one of the surviving material symbols of the Sikh Empire he consolidated at Lahore. Victoria and Albert Museum, London.
image.credit: Victoria and Albert Museum, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: Egypt was in the midst of the turbulent aftermath of the French occupation of 1798 to 1801, when Napoleon's expeditionary force under General Kleber (assassinated in Cairo in June 1800) and then General Menou had been driven out by a combined British and Ottoman effort. In 1804 the Albanian officer Muhammad Ali (Kavalali Mehmed Ali Pasha), who had come to Egypt with the Ottoman army sent to expel the French, was manoeuvring against the Mamluk beys under Muhammad al-Alfi and Uthman Bardisi and the succession of weak Ottoman viceroys; in May 1805 the ulama of Cairo led by Umar Makram would proclaim him wali of Egypt, beginning a dynasty that ruled the Nile valley until the abdication of King Faruq in 1952. In the Arabian peninsula the Wahhabi movement led by the House of Saud under Emir Saud bin Abdul-Aziz had captured Mecca in 1803 and Medina in 1805, sacking the tomb of the Prophet and alarming the Sublime Porte. In Iran, Fath-Ali Shah of the Qajar dynasty ruled from Tehran; the Russo-Persian War over the Caucasus had just begun.

image.caption: Muhammad Ali of Egypt, portrait in oil by the French painter Auguste Couder (1841). Commissioned for Louis-Philippe's historical museum at Versailles.
image.credit: Auguste Couder (1789 to 1873), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: Along the West African coast the transatlantic slave trade was approaching its peak: the British Atlantic trade, at the moment three years from its 1807 abolition, still shipped tens of thousands of enslaved Africans across the ocean each year through Bonny, Calabar, and Lagos. In the Sahel, the Fulani scholar and reformer Usman dan Fodio, born in 1754 in Gobir and trained in the Qadiriyya Sufi tradition, was preparing the jihad he formally launched on 21 February 1804 against Yunfa, the Hausa king of Gobir, after being driven from Degel; that movement, prosecuted with his son Muhammadu Bello and brother Abdullahi, would within a decade establish the Sokoto Caliphate across what is now northern Nigeria, the largest state in nineteenth-century sub-Saharan Africa. In the Cape Colony, briefly returned to Dutch Batavian Republic control from 1803, the Xhosa chiefdoms under Ngqika and the expanding Dutch-descended trekboer farmers continued the long series of frontier wars along the Great Fish River. In the Merina highlands of Madagascar, King Andrianampoinimerina was completing the unification of Imerina before his death in 1810.

image.caption: The Sankore Mosque at Timbuktu, once the great centre of West African Islamic learning; Usman dan Fodio's early nineteenth century Sokoto jihad drew on the same Sahelian scholarly tradition.
image.credit: Dr Ondrej Havelka (HistoriaSalutis), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The British penal colony at Sydney Cove, founded only in January 1788 under Governor Arthur Phillip, was recovering from the Castle Hill convict rebellion of 4 to 5 March 1804, in which around 260 mostly Irish political prisoners (transported after the 1798 United Irishmen rising) under Philip Cunningham had risen against the New South Wales Corps and were crushed at Rouse Hill by Major George Johnston, with 15 killed in the battle and 9 leaders hanged. Governor Philip Gidley King ruled a colony of about 7,000 people. In Aotearoa New Zealand the Maori remained wholly independent, still governing themselves by iwi and hapu; they had begun to acquire European muskets through whalers and sealers at the Bay of Islands, setting the stage for the intertribal Musket Wars of the 1810s to 1830s under leaders like Hongi Hika. Tasmania, then called Van Diemen's Land, was being surveyed for its first permanent European settlement at Hobart, founded in February 1804 under David Collins.

image.caption: The Castle Hill Rebellion of 4 to 5 March 1804, in which Irish political convicts rose against the New South Wales Corps outside Sydney and were crushed at Rouse Hill. Contemporary illustration, National Library of Australia.

figures.name: Napoleon Bonaparte
figures.role: First Consul of the French Republic who crowned himself Emperor of the French in Notre-Dame de Paris on 2 December 1804
figures.credit: Andrea Appiani (1805), Kunsthistorisches Museum Vienna, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Pope Pius VII
figures.role: Bishop of Rome who travelled from Rome to consecrate the new emperor and anoint him and Josephine with holy chrism
figures.credit: Sir Thomas Lawrence, via Google Art Project / Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Josephine de Beauharnais
figures.role: Empress of the French, crowned by her husband during the ceremony
figures.credit: Francois Gerard, via Google Art Project / Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Jacques-Louis David
figures.role: Painter and Napoleon's premier peintre, who recorded the coronation in a monumental canvas exhibited in 1808

figures.name: Charles Percier and Pierre Fontaine
figures.role: Imperial architects who redesigned the interior of Notre-Dame for the ceremony

images.caption: The Coronation of Napoleon by Jacques-Louis David, painted 1805 to 1807, depicts the moment the emperor sets the imperial crown on the kneeling Josephine. Musee du Louvre, Paris.
images.credit: Jacques-Louis David (1748 to 1825), via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: The Consecration of the Emperor Napoleon
